text:
The behaviors we now call psychopathy have a historical dimension that modern psychology tends to overlook. Before the 20th century medicalized these traits, they were often pathways to power and kingship. Check out David Graeber and Marshall Sahlins's "On Kings," which reveals how ritual sacrifice and transgressive violence were actually features, not bugs, of archaic political systems.

Here's what intrigues me: these ancient societies operated on a logic that seems alien to us now. The "stranger-king" didn't establish legitimacy through moral virtue, but through acts that violated normal human constraints - like ritual murder, incest, or killing family members. It's almost like a photo negative of our modern leadership ideals. These transgressions weren't scandals to be hidden; they were evidence of the ruler's superhuman status.

The arbitrary nature of royal violence - choosing ritual victims by chance - wasn't a sign of chaos but of divine power precisely because it transcended human reasoning. The system then "domesticated" this sacred violence through ritual, turning raw transgression into legitimate authority. 

Tom Holland's "Dominion" helps us see how Christianity attempted to subvert this system by making its central divine figure a victim of state violence rather than its wielder. But rather than eliminating the psychopathic-sacred complex entirely, Christianity ended up transforming it. The stranger-king became the conquistador/crusader - still wielding transgressive violence, but now justified through Christian mission as well as regulated by it (eg, the knight's commitment to always speak truth and defend the weak). This helps explain some deep contradictions in Christian civilization as expressed by the conquistador/crusader: how it could simultaneously uphold universal human dignity while preserving patterns of sacred violence in modified form.

This unveils something important about modern nation-states: it hasn't eliminated these ancient patterns but rationalized them to serve its own logic (kinda inspired by Foucault's arguments in "Madness and Civilization"). The stranger-king's divine violence becomes the state's monopoly of violence. What Graeber and Sahlins reveal as an ancient pattern of legitimizing power through transgression hasn't disappeared - it's been redistributed through seemingly rational institutions.

This might explain a paradox I've noticed in nation-states: how it simultaneously pathologizes "antisocial" behavior while rewarding classic psychopathic traits in leadership - grandiosity, manipulation, lack of empathy with power (I'm from the city of Rodrigo Duterte). The system needs these energies while denying their sacred origins, just as it requires the logic of sacrifice while denying its religious nature.

In a way, modern psychiatric definitions of psychopathy serve a similar function to ancient taboos: they help define and contain threatening forms of power while legitimizing others. The key difference is that we present this process as scientific rather than sacred - even as we continue transforming transgressive violence into legitimate authority, now through the seemingly neutral language of modern politics and mental health.

Those who want to preserve the status quo tend to be the ones whose stories are already told.

text:
There's a parallel unspoken history in the Philippines.

The colonialists must of course be the bad guys, so we are not taught that while the Spanish crown eliminated slave raiding in the colonized central and northern Philippines, they were unable to do so in the territories of the Sultanate of Sulu, which continued to raid southern and central Philippines. (The Spanish crown replaced full-time slavery with part-time slavery called "polo," a pre-capitalist version of income tax, wherein you work for the government without pay for a specified number of days.)

It was the Americans who finally defeated the Sulu Sultanate in the early 20th century. The Sultan tried to negotiate a continuation of their slave raiding, since this is their major source of income. The Americans said no.

text:
I've been planning a follow-up essay to Psychofauna Studies entitled "Life is Antifragile Information—Therefore Animism is True," wherein I expand on an old thread wherein I define life as antifragile information by presenting a mechanism: anything or anyone alive only needs the following:

- A record of its design: biological genome / sociological shared worldviews (taboos, ritual, identities) / institutional constraints and memory (laws, SOPs, hierarchies, traditions, histories) - this record carries the information into the future - "memory"
- An ability to vary its design: eg, via sexual reproduction for biology, random variation in premodern taboo-driven societies, designed modern societies (eg, the experiments of nation-states), creative destruction of corporations
- An ability to rearrange matter to increase the chances of survival and reproduction of the information's vessel: eg, animals hunting/hiding, plants attracting pollinators, humans building, propaganda of nation-states, technocapital rewarding humans that contribute to its expansion

The usual biologically-biased definition of life makes it unclear whether viruses are alive (biologists continue to argue about this), but if life is antifragile information, it is clear that viruses are alive. Nation-states, techocapital, corporations, movements, are also alive. Therefore animism is true. The derangements of animism (eg, ritual sacrifice) was due to lack of knowledge of these beings rather than inherent to the recognition of their existence, intelligence, and agency (however, anthropomorphizing them - ie attributing consciousness to them - was a mistake). Their subjugation under the One God in monotheism was a solution (a Thai also that Buddhism tamed Thai animism), as well as their death under modernity. But killing them (or being oblivious to their existence) also has its own dangers, as I argue in Psychofauna Studies.

text: Please allow me to share this significant milestone in my work as a scholar. My article, "The Scapegoat Mechanism in Southeast Asian Ritual, Myth, and Politics: From Mead and Bateson's Trance and Dance in Bali to Massacres in the Philippines and Indonesia" has been published in Michigan State University's Contagion Journal (sharing two sample pages). Readers of my novel Rajah Versus Conquistador might notice that the origin story of the binukot and baylan (chapter 5) is based on the Rangda and Barong myth I analyze in this article! The publication is quite timely because I applied the analysis on the role of killings in the rise to power of Rodrigo Duterte, who now awaits trial for crimes against humanity in The Hague. DM if you want the PDF (I can legally share it individually according to the fine print).

text:
Happy to share that I'll be one of the authors in The Philippines as Guest of Honour at the Frankfurt Book Fair 2025 this October. Hopefully we can bring Rajah Versus Conquistador to many publishers and their audiences across the world.

This year is special. This 500 year-old book fair picks one country every year as its "Guest of Honor." This year, the spotlight is on the Philippines. I'm glad to contribute a very Cebuano story from the heart of kabisay-an—and the pusod of the country—to the rich tapestry of stories that is the Philippines 🇵🇭

Thanks to @NBDBPhilippines . More on the event:

Filipino Stories. World Stage.

In less than 50 days, the Philippines will make history as the Guest of Honour at the Frankfurt Book Fair 2025!

In the fair's 500-year history, it turns its spotlight for the first time to the Philippines—all its 7,641 islands—lighting up the world’s biggest stage for books and ideas.  At the heart of it all is the Philippine Pavilion, our creative home in Frankfurt, bursting with pride and energy, and filled with 100+ talks, readings, and performances that showcase the brilliance of Filipino literature, arts, and culture.

This is more than a showcase. It’s a once-in-a-lifetime milestone where the Philippines steps into the global spotlight and tells its own stories in its own voice.

Made possible with the collaboration of the NBDB, the National Commission for Culture and the Arts, the Department of Foreign Affairs, and the Office of Senator Loren Legarda.
#FBM25 #ImaginationPeoplestheAir #booksphilippines #phlgoh2025

In the fair's 500-year history, it turns its spotlight for the first time to the Philippines—all its 7,641 islands—lighting up the world’s biggest stage for books and ideas.  At the heart of it all is the Philippine Pavilion, our creative home in Frankfurt, bursting with pride and energy, and filled with 100+ talks, readings, and performances that showcase the brilliance of Filipino literature, arts, and culture.

This is more than a showcase. It’s a once-in-a-lifetime milestone where the Philippines steps into the global spotlight and tells its own stories in its own voice.

Made possible with the collaboration of the NBDB, the National Commission for Culture and the Arts, the Department of Foreign Affairs, and the Office of Senator Loren Legarda.

text:
There's a line from a digital anthropology paper I'm reading rn: wherever people deploy digital media, there will be "circulations, reimaginings, magnifications, deletions" of cultural experience — but the precise ways these unfold can never be fully anticipated.

That's #roamcult in a sentence.

Nobody anticipated tattoos. Nobody anticipated secret cabals forming over a note-taking app. Nobody anticipated that tongue-in-cheek cult branding would license genuinely religious-level fervor. The pandemic cracked routines open and something rushed in.

The anthropologists say digital artifacts "engender new collectivities" — people whose sense of self and vocation are shaped significantly by the technology. 

I wrote my first book inside roamcult. My calling as a writer was partly shaped by a note-taking app and the people who treated it like a church.

Nobody caught roamcult by calmly evaluating features. You caught it from other people. Scrolling Twitter, you saw testimonials, life-changing claims, visible fervor — and you wanted what they seemed to have. Not just the tool. The clarity, the belonging, being at the edge.

Desire spread through contagion. And the tongue-in-cheek "cult" framing may have made it worse — the performer knows it's ironic, but the observer reads it as earnest, and this asymmetry fuels a spiral.

That's the puzzle I'm writing my MA thesis on. How did a note-taking app generate cult-like devotion, and what role did imitation play in that intensity?

I asked for someone to write this ethnography in 2022. Turns out I may be that someone.

text:
Which type of digital media community was #roamcult?

The obvious answer: vernacular culture — a group whose logic was organized around the specific properties of the tool (backlinks, daily notes, block refs).

However, it also bled into the third category. The tongue-in-cheek part of cult seemed like it was ocassionally forgotten. The prestige economy generated real economic exchange (courses, a $1M crowdfund). The medium didn't just reflect existing social practices, but spawned new ones that took on the texture of older institutions.

Vernacular culture that produced its own prosaics.

text: These amazing works of AI tend to be misleading. Eg this one "only" took 4 hours to make. But turns out the creator spent 20 years in the industry. When I went to a Runway ML FB group, all the uploaded vids looked like slop. We're in the chaotic phase of a restructuring of the world economy due to new tech. This has happened a few times in the past that we can kinda know the shape of game, even if it will be unique. It's tempting to jump into new lanes. If I were in my 20s, I'd try all the lanes and see which ones I like. But I already know the answer! More than sunk cost fallacy, its my past obsessions which guide me which frontier I should invest my time and brainspace in: scholarship, education, and perhaps storytelling (with the written word), but keeping tabs on adjacent lanes and players.
